sentences.text: Same place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have a bucket-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] terrible.
I cannot even want to remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know-- a day and a half or two days, you know, from-- we had to Auschwitz.
This time we don't know this name.
When we arrive--
The bucket to do-- do your pee and everything-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] terrible.
So--
Pardon me?
Terrible.
It's-- it's-- it's-- luckily this little baby was next to me, and needed sort of a concentrate because she was crying.
And then I heard my father davening, and-- and my sister, and we don't know what-- you know, where they taking us.
I can not remember if I used it.
I honest with you.
I just cannot remember.
Maybe we-- maybe we cannot go through [? with ?] do it.
I really cannot remember.
So anyhow, when we arrived to Auschwitz, there was men with the stripe.
Look like pajamas-- we don't know that they are Jewish people.
When the door opened, you don't feel nothing, because they said, rush, rush, rush.
Head out.
Head out.
Leave everything.
We couldn't take it with us-- nothing-- what we prepared for the 30 kilogram.
You know.
Pardon me?
Very early morning.
Very early morning, and it was windy and cold and-- head out, head out.
And should have a line, and the music playing.
So anyhow, we was in a line.
Then right away, they said in German, to men, this side, woman, this side.
And my mother and my father went with the men.
My mother was front of us.
And it was very windy and was a royal blue shawl.
And even-- I want to put top of, and I want to kiss her.
And Mengele-- at this time I don't know that's Mengele-- I really wish to learn to paint in portrait, because I could see him like I see you.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] You don't need to say goodbye tonight.
You're going to be together.
I never saw her.
I never her-- saw my father.
And my cousin hold a little boy, because it was vacationing for Passover, and she went same way, like my mother-- the gas chamber.
What do I-- last thing I told to her?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, and-- and just-- just front of me, and this man doesn't let-- just I put the shawl over.
And then we went-- this was a gate, and we went there like marching.
And was a fence, and people was behind the fence with nightgown or-- or short and no hair, and look like the mentally people, so we talking quietly.
They take us to the mental place, you know.
But they took us to a room, and they said, who speak German?
And to yell to take off everything, just hold the glasses and the shoes.
And the shoes needed to put in some kind-- probably some chlorine or something.
And we get some shower.
We don't know it going to be gas or water, for a second or two second.
They took us to the other room.
This time, all women, and most of them young people.
And in the room, all over German, young SS laughing and things.
And they took off your hair, underneath, and here.
That's such a humiliating thing, such an unhuman thing.
This I never forgot.
And then we went out.
You know, everything was rush, rush, rush.
And they give us some-- some garbage or something to put it on.
Pardon me?
What I got?
What I think.
What I think-- I was think about-- just couldn't think, really.
And people who was there-- I told you before-- we was-- that was from ghetto before our transport.
And then they said, you shouldn't drink water.
There was sort of a long thing when you can open up that comes some water, because this is already something in it that you will get sick.
Then somebody, all of the sudden, yelling my name in a tiny little window-- Ilona, Ilona.
Who was with us?
This boy in the ghetto and came from Tokaj give the handkerchief because his sister was with us and a piece-- about this piece-- of bread.
And I don't see him.
The year 1945, almost '46, he came home later than we came back, youknow, from the concentration camp.
His name?
Was [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took us in by the evening.
That was the [GERMAN], the [GERMAN].
And took us to a barrack with about more than 1,000 people.
The barrack-- I remember the number was 19.
But I cannot remember the name.
There were the three how they call?

Bunk bed, and I think we was at the middle or the first 13 people-- six these way and six this way with, I think, one blanket [? or ?] [? two. ?] And middle of the night, [INAUDIBLE], zahlappell, probably you heard about it.
You know, they're counting us.
But they come to counting, not right away.
They was middle of the night.
But maybe I should tell some-- I don't know how many weeks we was in Auschwitz.
But we was young.
However, people in the barrack like shouted [INAUDIBLE] a woman, they get crazy.
Soon as somebody-- then they took the crematorium.
But when we go out front of the barrack, it was so cold.
And we stand next to each other.
So we talked about food and singing.
And took me 52 years, when I find it out this tune name, what we were singing.
We make this song.
You will read [?
Marta ?] books, you know, because if I tell you Hungarian, you don't understand.
You want I-- I--
It's silly, really, but a week ago Thursday-- I told you I teaching, still volunteer-- so five or six, you know, senior, asking about the Holocaust and things, you know.
And I said, oh, I just want to tell you nice things.
We even make some parody sung-- song in the ghetto.
But I cannot tell you what the tune.
So one of the women is a very good musician, you know, and a Jewish girl.
So I-- I sing it.
She said, Ilona, this is 'Lili Marleen' song.
So it took me 52, and, you know, I just couldn't.
But we made it.
I don't know the real Hungarian version.
But what-- what we make it up.

[NON-ENGLISH SINGING] The song is about we are outside, and we talking about food, and the freezing, and when the German came and counting us, and always miss two, three children, and how they said then we have to kneel, and every 10 get killed.
But then the gong, you know, hear is so good, because we just dream, because we're going in and going to have nice warm food and warm blanket.
And we think about how going to grow our hair-- something like that.
But [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The hardest thing was, in Auschwitz, when they selecting us.
Always selected naked, and you hold your hand and go around.
And we three of us doesn't look like sister.
My youngest one blonde, my middle one red, and I was dark haired like her.
And she [? wore-- ?] my oldest one wore glasses.
So once we get selected, two of us.
She doesn't.
So we needed to somehow running to the other round.
Then we should together.
And finally, they selected three of us and a couple other youngster.
And they took us to an open wagon.
I think we went to Torun, if I could remember.
What we eat?
They bring a-- a bucket of some kind liquid, green.
Something was in it, because we don't menstruate.
You know, whole time when we was in-- we was [? all ?] concentration camp we didn't.
And after, when we came back, I get a couple shots, and then after-- I never think about we're going to have children.
Yeah, but not so sick.
Like-- the other hardest thing was when we're zahlappell, my sister, who's older, she was the strongest one in us.
But because we standing in the hot sun, she fainted.
And when we saw the German come, we needed to hold her up.
And finally some woman tell her how she should do.
And then one zahlappell, I get such a heat because I get some long schmattas or something.
And I cut it off, and here was open.
So I cut to a babushka to have it, and cut it here, so he saw it.
Then he hit me, you know, to do in this kind of deal.
But for the blockalteste needed some slippers.
And my younger sister and me-- I don't know-- with a knife, we cut and we make by hand some slippers for her.
Then we get a little extra bed for [INAUDIBLE] lager sister.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
What?
That was the whole deal in Auschwitz.
You know, zahlappell and standing outside till when they counting you.
And when they missed you counting was disappear, then we needed to [? kneel. ?] And once, Elizabeth said to me we went to help to bring the food.
I cannot remember.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after, when we get to this small lager, we cut the grass and make-- cover the bunker for the German.
But the American people came, so took us back.
And then we came back to Auschwitz.
People said the worst thing about-- they took you to crematory.
Then he can selected us.
And we when to Stutthof.
Latrine-- you know what this means, a latrine?
That's-- but you cannot go when you needed to go.
Either you hold, or-- And they took our schmatta, the clothes, to-- because we have lice, I think, when we came back-- not the-- not the first couple weeks in Auschwitz.
You know, and one's like-- who was with us, who gave the bread, and niece was with us.
And I saw her dress, you know, so I want to tell the blockalteste [INAUDIBLE].
She hit me so I still have the mark, because we was nothing on us.
This one, they needed later on, operate on, in Magdeburg, but not in the hospital.
Because if they send you to the river, then they said they sent you to the river.
They called the first aid, and they took you to the crematorium.
But was a doctor, you know, in Magdeburg.
Because I want to take this dress to this little girl.
This little girl was 15, and we want to-- she shouldn't look like so young.
With- she had a wooden-- this wide-- stick.
That's nothing new.
This-- I mean, that was not only me who got hit, because she was the blockalteste.
She needed to make orders.
And we wasn't human.
We really wasn't human.
Some of them was Polish.
Some of them-- we have to understand them.
When we work in Magdeburg and we was in the woman's lager, you know, not-- not the 1,000 people in a lager.
So we was in-- three of us in one bunk bed.
Above us was three Polish girl that aren't Jewish girl.
And she said, you know, when you ate home the nice cake, they build this barrack for you.
So, you know, here now we are [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] human.
That time, we couldn't think about-- you know, we was like animals.
Did you know about Jewish holidays?
One holiday I remember-- Yom Kippur-- one woman stood up on-- not chair.
I don't know what-- what they call-- maybe just some stone-- and sing the Kol Nidre.
I cannot remember if this in Torun or Auschwitz when we came back or Stutthof.
I don't know.
In Torun?
I cannot remember.
No.
We was there not long.
And we came back, and people think, you know, we came back to Auschwitz, then we died, then selected again.
Just I told you-- this green square we needed to cut out from-- from the grass and cover the bunker for the Germans.
I don't know.
Two weeks, three weeks-- I don't know.
Only things I remember-- after, we went Stutthof, and we was there a couple weeks also.
Then we came back again and selected us.
And this time, we get some new things.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I remember coat, which have little fur.
And I don't know how the Polish or Czechoslovakian girls who get selected, they had more bread or something.
My sister and me, we change into our nice coat to their old one.
And we get the piece separate from-- I don't know how they did it.
But they took us to Magdeburg.
This is Germany.
One thing I remember.
We was in Magdeburg already Christmastime.
You know, so we was-- I don't know how many weeks before Christmas, just how I told there was a nice [NON-ENGLISH] for my machine.
And when was Christmas Eve, she said, what can I wish my kind, my child?
Just you should be free and healthy.
This-- we get every day white gloves in Magdeburg.
It was-- we was in-- we was like animals, really, because for a piece of bread, people was ready to kill.
And so many people get crazy, literally crazy.
And people who you saw in the ghetto-- it was an educated woman, one of the lawyers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's really--
Because in Auschwitz, we was in the C Lager.
There was a big fence that wasn't supervised.
Some people want to escape through the fence, that was electric, and hang up there.
Then one day, the other side of the fence, family with children.
And I don't know where they come from.
But whole group of children, family, not Hungarian.
I don't know what nationality.
Next day, nobody was there.
And what we heard-- they took the crematorium.
Pardon me?
I really don't-- I-- I cannot.
I saw in the Spielberg film.
That's one of the reason I would like to go back.
I think I could handle now.
I'm not sure.
But--
Very much.
And you know something, girls and gentlemen, everybody have to believe something.
I believe that he and she know what we talking about.
I really-- I feel close that-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They raise five daughters, and they wasn't able to be any of their daughter at [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I helped the Jewish [? bookstore, ?] one man came over.
Maybe you read it, this book.
I don't know why bad things happen, good people.
That's a big question.
Many people who came back from the concentration camp, they don't believe in God, because how could happen?
But I believe it.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] So we work in Magdeburg.
The nicest things was when was bombing the-- Just-- just one more question-- did you can see anybody die in Auschwitz?
We couldn't see that, because they took right away, you know.
And so many people was in one-- even one bunk bed.
Then-- then-- you was close-- the 11 lager sisters who we are, and so we watch ourselves.
And-- it's--
[SIGHS] Because we wasn't treated human, and we don't feel we are really human being.
And, how could this happen?
I think only kept us going when we was so close to each other body to warm each other.
And we talked about food or what we're going to make or singing this.
And--
So otherwise, maybe if we don't have this, maybe we would be-- all be crazy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you know, front of the barrack always was-- you know, the big [INAUDIBLE] lager front of-- they have an orchestra who played all the time.
We will change tapes now.
